lines.greek: ἀθάνατοι βάλλουσι καὶ ὡς τελέεσθαι ὀίω,
οὔτε τι μάντις ἐὼν οὔτʼ οἰωνῶν σάφα εἰδώς.
οὔ τοι ἔτι δηρόν γε φίλης ἀπὸ πατρίδος αἴης
ἔσσεται, οὐδʼ εἴ πέρ τε σιδήρεα δέσματʼ ἔχῃσιν·
φράσσεται ὥς κε νέηται, ἐπεὶ πολυμήχανός ἐστιν.
ἀλλʼ ἄγε μοι τόδε εἰπὲ καὶ ἀτρεκέως κατάλεξον,
εἰ δὴ ἐξ αὐτοῖο τόσος πάϊς εἰς Ὀδυσῆος.
αἰνῶς μὲν κεφαλήν τε καὶ ὄμματα καλὰ ἔοικας
κείνῳ, ἐπεὶ θαμὰ τοῖον ἐμισγόμεθʼ ἀλλήλοισιν,
πρίν γε τὸν ἐς Τροίην ἀναβήμεναι, ἔνθα περ ἄλλοι
Ἀργείων οἱ ἄριστοι ἔβαν κοίλῃς ἐνὶ νηυσίν·
ἐκ τοῦ δʼ οὔτʼ Ὀδυσῆα ἐγὼν ἴδον οὔτʼ ἔμʼ ἐκεῖνος.
τὴν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
τοιγὰρ ἐγώ τοι, ξεῖνε, μάλʼ ἀτρεκέως ἀγορεύσω.
μήτηρ μέν τέ μέ φησι τοῦ ἔμμεναι, αὐτὰρ ἐγώ γε
οὐκ οἶδʼ· οὐ γάρ πώ τις ἑὸν γόνον αὐτὸς ἀνέγνω.
ὡς δὴ ἐγώ γʼ ὄφελον μάκαρός νύ τευ ἔμμεναι υἱὸς
ἀνέρος, ὃν κτεάτεσσιν ἑοῖς ἔπι γῆρας ἔτετμε.
νῦν δʼ ὃς ἀποτμότατος γένετο θνητῶν ἀνθρώπων,
τοῦ μʼ ἔκ φασι γενέσθαι, ἐπεὶ σύ με τοῦτʼ ἐρεείνεις.

interlinear: the-immortals cast-it, and as to-be-fulfilled I-think,
neither a-seer being, nor of-bird-signs clearly knowing.
not for-you still long indeed from-his-dear native land
will-he-be, not-even if iron bonds he-should-have;
he-will-contrive how he-may-return, since of-many-devices he-is.
but come, to-me this tell, and truly recount:
whether indeed from him-himself so-tall a-child you-are, of-Odysseus.
wondrously in-head and in-eyes fair you-resemble
that-man, since often in-such-wise we-mingled with-one-another,
before he to Troy went-up, where indeed the-others,
of-the-Argives the-best, went in-hollow ships;
and-since then neither Odysseus have-I seen, nor me that-man.'
and-her in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Therefore I to-you, stranger, very truly will-declare.
my-mother indeed says me of-him to-be, but I myself
do-not know; for not-yet has-anyone his-own begetting himself known.
would that I were of-some blessed man the-son,
of-a-man whom amid his-possessions old-age overtook.
but-now he-who most-luckless was-born of-mortal men,
of-him me they-say to-be-sprung, since you me this ask.'
literal: the immortals cast it, and as I think it shall be fulfilled,
though I am no seer, nor one who reads bird-signs clearly.
Not much longer will he be far from his dear native land,
not even if bonds of iron hold him;
he will contrive a way to return, for he is of many devices.
But come, tell me this, and recount it truly:
are you indeed, tall as you are, the son of Odysseus himself?
Wondrously do you resemble him about the head and the fair eyes,
for often did we meet with one another in this way,
before he went up to Troy, where the others also,
the best of the Argives, went in their hollow ships.
Since then I have not seen Odysseus, nor he me.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered her:
'Therefore, stranger, I will declare it to you very truly.
My mother says that I am his; but for myself
I do not know, for no man ever yet knew his own begetting.
Would that I were the son of some blessed man
whom old age overtook amid his own possessions.
But now, of him who was born most luckless of mortal men,
of him they say I am sprung, since you ask me this.'
translations.elizabethan:
Not long shall he be kept from his own land,
not though they bind him fast with iron bands.
He will contrive his coming, being a man
of many devices. But now tell me true:
art thou indeed his son, and grown so tall,
the son of that Odysseus? Wondrous like
thou art about the head and the fair eyes.
For often did we come together thus,
before he sailed for Troy, where all the best
among the Argives went in hollow ships.
Since then I have not seen him, nor he me."
Then prudent Telemachus made answer thus:
"Stranger, I will declare it to thee plain.
My mother says I am his son. For me,
I do not know. No man yet ever knew
of his own getting. Would that I had been
the son of some blest man whom age o'ertook
among his own possessions. But they say
I am the son of him who was born of all
mortals the most unlucky. Thou didst ask."
translations.modernist:
He will not be away from home much longer. Not even if they have him in iron. He will work out a way back. He is a man of many devices.

Now tell me this, and tell it straight. Are you his son? You are tall enough. You look like him about the head and the eyes. It is remarkable. We used to meet like this, before he went up to Troy, where the best of the Argives went in the hollow ships. I have not seen him since. He has not seen me.

Then Telemachus answered her. He had sense.

"I will tell you plainly, stranger. My mother says I am his son. I do not know myself. No man ever knew who fathered him. I wish I were the son of some lucky man who grew old among his own things. They tell me I am the son of the unluckiest man ever born. That is your answer, since you asked."
translations.storybook:
He will not be away from home for very much longer. Not even if they tie him up with iron chains! He will think of a way to come back, for he is the cleverest man alive at getting himself out of trouble.

But now tell me truly: are you really his son? You have grown so tall! You look wonderfully like him about the head and the eyes. We used to meet often in the old days, before he sailed away to Troy with all the best of the Argives in their hollow ships. I have never seen him since, and he has never seen me.

Then sensible young Telemachus answered her.

"I will tell you honestly, stranger. My mother says that I am his son. But I do not really know, for no one has ever been quite sure who his own father was. Oh, how I wish I were the son of some lucky man who grew old peacefully among his own things! But they tell me I am the son of the unluckiest man who was ever born. And that is your answer, since you asked me."

murray.text: He will contrive a way to return, for he is a man of many devices. But come, tell me this and declare it truly, whether indeed, tall as thou art, thou art the son of Odysseus himself. Wondrously like his are thy head and beautiful eyes; for full often did we consort with one another

before he embarked for the land of Troy, whither others, too, the bravest of the Argives, went in their hollow ships. But since that day neither have I seen Odysseus, nor he me.” Then wise Telemachus answered her: “Therefore of a truth, stranger, will I frankly tell thee all.

My mother says that I am his child; but I know not, for never yet did any man of himself know his own parentage. Ah, would that I had been the son of some blest man, whom old age overtook among his own possessions. But now of him who was the most ill-fated of mortal men

they say that I am sprung, since thou askest me of this.” Then the goddess, flashing-eyed Athena, answered him: “Surely, then, no nameless lineage have the gods appointed for thee in time to come, seeing that Penelope bore thee such as thou art. But come, tell me this and declare it truly.
